document.source_title: From PCOS to PMOS: understanding the reasons for name change

claims.claim_text: The Endocrine Society announced that PCOS would henceforth be known as PMOS.
claims.evidence_text: The Endocrine Society, a global network of people dealing with hormones, announced that PCOS (Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome) would henceforth be known as PMOS (Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome).

claims.claim_text: The term PCOS was deemed 'inaccurate' as it relied only on the presence of ovarian cysts.
claims.evidence_text: The authors of the paper Helena J. Teede et al argued that the term PCOS was ‘inaccurate’ as it relied only on the presence of ovarian cysts.

claims.claim_text: PCOS affects an estimated 170 million women during their reproductive years.
claims.evidence_text: The significance lies not only in the fact that the definition has broadened the panel of factors that will facilitate better diagnosis, but also that PCOS affects a staggering number of women — it is estimated that the syndrome affects 170 million women during their reproductive years alone.

entities.name: The Endocrine Society
entities.description: A global network of people dealing with hormones.
entities.evidence_text: The Endocrine Society, a global network of people dealing with hormones, announced that PCOS (Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome) would henceforth be known as PMOS (Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome).

entities.name: The Lancet
entities.description: A medical journal that published an article discussing the name change.
entities.evidence_text: As an article in The Lancet pointed out, the new name emerged out of a multistep global consensus process over the years.

events.event: Announcement of name change from PCOS to PMOS
events.evidence_text: The Endocrine Society, a global network of people dealing with hormones, announced that PCOS (Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome) would henceforth be known as PMOS (Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome).

timelines.milestone: Integration of PMOS into international guidelines
timelines.evidence_text: The Lancet paper lists an eight-stage process of global implementation of the new name, leading up to integration into the international guidelines in 2028.

citations.title: From PCOS to PMOS: understanding the reasons for name change

source_credibility.reason: The article discusses a significant change in medical terminology supported by expert opinions.
